1990.assessment:
Formal unemployment in Senegal ~4-5% in 1990. However, formal unemployment
statistics drastically understate actual underemployment and poverty. The
vast majority of Senegalese worked in subsistence agriculture or the
informal urban sector. Youth unemployment and urban underemployment were
severe. Low formal rate is misleading.

1990.assessment:
Senegal maintained full diplomatic relations with all major powers and was an active
participant in international institutions. A founding member of the OAU (1963), ECOWAS
(1975), and the Islamic Conference Organisation, Senegal had longstanding close ties
with France, the United States, and China. Dakar served as a major African diplomatic
hub hosting regional headquarters of international organizations.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook - Senegal; Keesing's Record of World Events 1990

under UN Security Council authorization, reinforcing its standing as a reliable
multilateral partner. Relations with France, the US, and regional partners remained strong.

FDI to Senegal was minimal in 1990, below 0.5% of GDP. The country had limited
extractive resources attracting foreign capital; investment was concentrated in
phosphate mining and tourism. Political stability under Diouf provided a foundation
but the economy was small and undiversified.

2017.assessment:
FDI continued rising to approximately 3.4% of GDP as hydrocarbon development
investments accelerated. Woodside Petroleum, BP, and Cairn Energy all increased
capital commitments for the Sangomar and GTA fields.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI net inflows % GDP, Senegal 2017; Woodside Petroleum annual report 2017

2018.assessment:
FDI rose to approximately 3.7% of GDP, among the highest levels in Senegalese
history. Hydrocarbon development and associated infrastructure drove investment.
Sall's re-election in February 2019 anticipated.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI net inflows % GDP, Senegal 2018; UNCTAD World Investment Report 2019

2019.assessment:
FDI reached approximately 4.2% of GDP, driven by accelerating hydrocarbon project
development expenditure. Sangomar and GTA fields entered intensive development phase
with major capital commitments from Woodside and BP.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI net inflows % GDP, Senegal 2019; Woodside annual report 2019; BP annual report 2019

2020.assessment:
FDI fell to approximately 3% of GDP due to COVID-19 pandemic disruptions which
halted or delayed construction on major oil and gas projects. Global FDI fell
sharply in 2020; Senegal was not insulated from this trend.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI net inflows % GDP, Senegal 2020; UNCTAD World Investment Report 2021

2021.assessment:
FDI recovered to approximately 3.2% of GDP as oil and gas project construction
resumed post-COVID. Sangomar and GTA fields back on track. Political unrest
had limited impact on major investment commitments.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI net inflows % GDP, Senegal 2021

2022.assessment:
FDI approximately 3.5% of GDP. Final construction phase investments for the Sangomar
oilfield (targeted for 2024 production) and GTA LNG project continued at high levels.
Russian-Ukraine war boosted interest in African energy alternatives.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI net inflows % GDP, Senegal 2022; Woodside annual report 2022

2023.assessment:
FDI approximately 3% of GDP. Political uncertainty surrounding the election
postponement crisis (February 2023) may have caused some investor caution, but
hydrocarbon project investments continued. The Sangomar field completed construction
ahead of June 2024 first oil.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI estimate for Senegal 2023; Woodside Sangomar project timeline

2024.assessment:
FDI jumped to approximately 4.8% of GDP (estimate) with Sangomar oilfield commencing
first oil production in June 2024, attracting final investment disbursements and
operational capital. The GTA LNG project continued large-scale capital spending.
Faye's election did not deter investment; the new government renegotiated petroleum
contracts to improve Senegal's revenue share.

sources.citation: Woodside Senegal Sangomar first oil announcement, June 2024; IMF Senegal Article IV 2024

2025.assessment:
FDI expected to remain elevated at approximately 4% of GDP with Sangomar and GTA
in production phase. New government contract renegotiations introduced some uncertainty
but Senegal remained an attractive destination for energy-sector investment.

sources.citation: IMF Senegal Article IV 2025 estimate; African Development Bank Senegal country profile

1990.assessment:
France maintained a permanent military base in Dakar (Base navale de Dakar / Forces
françaises du Cap Vert), the oldest French overseas military installation in Africa,
dating to the colonial era. The base includes naval facilities and a small garrison.
This is a consensual, invited French presence under bilateral defense agreements.
The presence is relatively small (approximately 1,000-1,200 personnel historically)
and not viewed as undermining Senegalese sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1990; French Ministry of Defense

1991.assessment:
French military base in Dakar continued under bilateral defense agreements. French
forces deployed briefly to neighboring states during Gulf War logistics but Senegal
itself was not a major operational hub for the Gulf War. Limited invited French presence.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1991

French military base in Dakar continued under bilateral defense agreements. France

French military base in Dakar continued under bilateral defense agreements. France

French military base in Dakar continued under bilateral defense agreements. Senegal

French military base in Dakar continued under bilateral defense agreements. US

French military base in Dakar continued under bilateral defense agreements. As

2022.assessment: French military base in Dakar continued under bilateral defense agreements.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2022

2023.assessment:
French military base in Dakar continued under bilateral defense agreements. Opposition
figure Ousmane Sonko and the Pastef party vocally opposed the French military presence
as part of a broader sovereignty platform, signaling possible future policy change.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2023; Pastef party platform on French bases

2024.assessment:
Following the March 2024 election of Faye, the new Faye/Sonko government announced
the closure of French military bases in Senegal, including the Dakar naval base,
as part of a sovereignty-centered foreign policy. France confirmed this in
November 2024. The bases were being wound down by year-end 2024, marking a
significant shift in Senegal's foreign military posture from invited limited
presence to no foreign presence.

sources.citation: Reuters: Senegal to close French military bases, November 2024; French Ministry of Defense confirmation

2025.assessment:
French military bases formally closed in early 2025 following the Faye government's
decision announced in late 2024. Senegal transitioned to full sovereignty with no
permanent foreign military presence on its territory for the first time since
independence. The new government maintained security cooperation with France and other
partners through non-basing arrangements.

sources.citation: French Ministry of Defense; Senegalese Ministry of Foreign Affairs, 2025

1990.assessment:
Senegal experienced modest internal displacement due to the Casamance conflict
(1982-ongoing), primarily in the southern Casamance region. Cross-border refugee
flows into Guinea-Bissau and Gambia were limited. Senegal also hosted small numbers
of Mauritanian refugees from the 1989 Senegal-Mauritania border crisis, in which
ethnic violence led to mass expulsions. Overall displacement was below 1% of
population per year.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1990; Human Rights Watch report on Senegal-Mauritania crisis 1989-90

1991.assessment:
Continued low-level displacement from Casamance conflict. Senegal hosted
approximately 50,000 Mauritanian refugees following the 1989 expulsions.
Net population displacement below 1% annually.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1991

1992.assessment: Continued low-level displacement from Casamance conflict. Net flows minimal.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1992

1993.assessment:
Casamance conflict continued to generate modest displacement. The July 1993 ceasefire
allowed some returns of displaced civilians. Senegal remained a net refugee-receiving
country for Mauritanians and other West Africans.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1993

1994.assessment:
Continued modest displacement from Casamance. The ceasefire held intermittently
but did not fully stop displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1994

1995.assessment:
Continued low-level Casamance displacement. Senegal received Liberian and Sierra
Leonean refugees from the Sierra Leone civil war.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1995

1996.assessment:
Continued low-level Casamance displacement. The Casamance ceasefire reduced new
outflows significantly.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1996

1997.assessment:
Casamance conflict continued at low level. Guinea-Bissau civil conflict (1997-1998)
began generating refugees into Senegal.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1997

1998.assessment:
Senegal received approximately 15,000-20,000 Guinea-Bissau refugees from the civil
conflict there (1998-1999). Combined with ongoing Casamance displaced and Mauritanian
refugees still in country, total displaced exceeded prior years slightly but remained
below 1% of Senegal's population.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1998; Guinea-Bissau civil war documentation

1999.assessment:
Guinea-Bissau conflict resolution allowed return of most refugees. Casamance
conflict continued at low levels. Overall displacement flows minimal.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 1999

2000.assessment:
Continued low-level displacement from Casamance. Overall flows minimal and stable.
Senegal's peaceful democratic transition had no displacement effect.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2000

2001.assessment:
Low-level Casamance displacement continued. Senegal hosted approximately 20,000-25,000
refugees from various countries (Mauritania, Sierra Leone, Liberia, Guinea-Bissau).

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2001

2002.assessment: Continued low-level displacement from Casamance. Overall population movements minimal.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2002

2003.assessment: Continued low-level Casamance displacement. No major new outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2003

2004.assessment:
The December 2004 Ziguinchor peace accord reduced Casamance displacement significantly.
Some returns of displaced persons to Casamance began. Overall flows minimal and trending
toward net returns.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2004; Ziguinchor Accord December 2004

2005.assessment:
Very minimal displacement. Some Casamance displaced persons returning to their
home areas following the peace accord.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2005

2006.assessment:
Very minimal displacement. Casamance peace holding; displaced persons continuing
to return.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2006

2007.assessment:
Low-level displacement resumed after Casamance ceasefire broke down in 2007.
Limited new displacement from renewed Casamance incidents.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2007

2008.assessment:
Low-level displacement from Casamance continued. Senegal also hosted growing numbers
of Guinean and other West African refugees as regional instability persisted.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2008

2009.assessment:
Continued low-level Casamance displacement. Senegal continued to receive refugees
from the West African subregion.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2009

2010.assessment:
Continued low-level Casamance displacement. Senegal hosted approximately 20,000
refugees in total from neighboring countries.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2010

2011.assessment:
Low-level Casamance displacement; political unrest in Senegal itself (M23 protests
against Wade) generated no significant refugee flows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2011

2012.assessment:
Continued low-level Casamance displacement. Mali coup in March 2012 and subsequent
Tuareg/jihadist conflict generated Malian refugees; Senegal received several thousand
Malian refugees. Peaceful Sall election did not cause displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2012; UNHCR Mali situation report 2012

2013.assessment:
Casamance conflict declining toward resolution; fewer new displacements. Senegal
continued to host Malian, Mauritanian, and other West African refugees.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2013

2014.assessment:
Casamance conflict effectively over from 2014; significant returns of displaced
persons to Casamance villages. Senegal continued to host approximately 14,000-20,000
refugees from neighboring countries. Net flows: returns dominant over new displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2014; UNHCR Senegal country operations profile

2015.assessment:
Very low displacement. Casamance essentially peaceful. Senegal hosted approximately
14,000 recognized refugees mainly from Mauritania, Mali, and Guinea-Bissau.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2015; UNHCR Senegal country profile

2016.assessment:
Very low displacement. Senegal stable; Casamance essentially peaceful. Approximately
14,000-15,000 refugees in Senegal from neighboring countries.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2016

2017.assessment:
Very low displacement. Senegal's ECOMIG intervention to resolve the Gambia crisis
did not cause significant Senegalese population displacement. Some Gambian refugees
entered Senegal initially during the Jammeh standoff.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2017; UNHCR Gambia situation update

2018.assessment:
Very low displacement. Senegal remained stable with minimal new outflows.
Approximately 14,000-16,000 refugees in Senegal.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2018

2019.assessment:
Very low displacement. Senegal stable; no significant new outflows or inflows
beyond normal refugee hosting levels.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2019

2020.assessment:
Very low displacement. COVID-19 restricted cross-border movements. Senegal continued
to host approximately 14,000-16,000 refugees from the wider region.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2020

2021.assessment:
Very low displacement. Political protests (Sonko) did not generate significant
displacement. Senegal continued to host approximately 15,000 refugees.
Sahel instability in neighboring Mali and Burkina Faso did not yet create
significant spill-over displacement into Senegal.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2021

2022.assessment:
Very low displacement. Senegal remained stable. Growing Sahel insecurity in
Mali and Burkina Faso did not significantly spill over into Senegal, though
Senegal monitored border areas closely.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2022

2023.assessment:
Very low displacement. Political tensions (election postponement, protests) did not
generate significant population displacement. Senegal continued to host approximately
16,000-18,000 refugees. No major outflows from Senegal.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistical Yearbook 2023

2024.assessment:
Very low displacement. Peaceful third democratic transfer to Faye generated no
refugee flows. Senegal remained one of the most stable refugee-hosting countries
in West Africa, continuing to host approximately 16,000-18,000 registered refugees.

sources.citation: UNHCR Senegal country profile 2024

2025.assessment:
Very low displacement. Senegal remained stable with no significant new outflows.
Continued hosting of approximately 16,000-20,000 refugees from neighboring countries.

sources.citation: UNHCR Senegal country profile 2025

1990.assessment:
Senegal faced no international sanctions in 1990. As a stable, pro-Western democracy
aligned with France and active in international institutions, Senegal maintained full
economic relations globally. The country was a key partner for France, the United States,
and international financial institutions.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC sanctions lists; no Senegal entries

No sanctions applied. Senegal remained a reliable Western partner and active participant
in multilateral institutions including the UN, OAU, and ECOWAS.

No sanctions applied. Senegal continued as a stable, cooperative partner with the
international community.

No sanctions applied. Senegal actively cooperated with IMF structural adjustment programs,
demonstrating international economic integration rather than isolation.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC; EU Council sanctions database

No sanctions applied. Senegal implemented the CFA franc devaluation in coordination
with France and the IMF, reinforcing its image as a cooperative international partner.
The devaluation was praised by the Bretton Woods institutions.

1990.assessment:
Senegal in 1990 under Diouf had moderate civil liberties. It was a
Partially Free country with multiparty elections but significant restrictions
on press and political activity. State media dominant. Casamance conflict
led to some emergency measures in the south. FH CL=4 (inverted: 57.1).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1991, Senegal

1991.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in 1991 under Diouf. Senegal maintained
multiparty system but PS dominance limited genuine political competition.
Private press existed but state media dominant. FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1992, Senegal

1992.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in 1992. Growing independent press.
Political parties active. Some restrictions on assembly and expression
still present. Senegal's civil liberties above most of West Africa but
well below full freedom. FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1993, Senegal

1993.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in election year 1993. Presidential
election (Diouf re-elected) disputed but conducted. Casamance conflict
led to some emergency restrictions in south. Press moderately free.
FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1994, Senegal

1994.assessment:
CFA franc devaluation (January 1994) prompted large protests; government
used force to restrict assembly. Civil liberties modestly constrained.
Press allowed to operate but some harassment. Casamance restrictions.
FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995, Senegal

1995.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in 1995. Private press expanding.
Political parties active. Freedom of assembly generally respected outside
Casamance. FH CL=4, placing Senegal in the Partly Free category.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996, Senegal

1996.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in 1996. ONEL electoral reform improved
political freedoms. Private newspapers flourishing. Radio expanding.
Some restrictions on assembly and harassment of journalists continued.
FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997, Senegal

1997.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in 1997. Press freedom improving with
multiple private newspapers and some independent radio. Political
opposition active. Some restrictions from Casamance conflict. FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998, Senegal

1998.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in 1998. Legislative elections (May
1998) conducted with reasonable freedom. Press coverage of elections
relatively free. Political competition improving. FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999, Senegal

1999.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in pre-election 1999. Open debate about
who should succeed Diouf. Multiple candidates active. Press moderately
free. Setting the stage for the democratic transition. FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000, Senegal

2000.assessment:
Civil liberties improved significantly in 2000, the regime change year.
Senegal upgraded to Free status after first peaceful transfer of power
since independence. Press freedom improved, political assembly unrestricted,
independent civil society flourishing. FH CL=3 (inverted: 71.4).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001, Senegal

2001.assessment:
Civil liberties well-established under Wade in 2001. New constitution
with strong rights provisions. Free press, civil society, assembly rights
protected. Senegal a model for West African democracy. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002, Senegal

2002.assessment:
Civil liberties well-protected in 2002. Free press, independent civil
society, religious freedom (Sufi brotherhoods prominent), assembly rights
all functioning. Senegal among best civil liberties in West Africa. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003, Senegal

2003.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained at good level under Wade. Free press active.
Civil society organizations thriving. Assembly and association rights
respected. Senegal's civil liberty record strong for the region. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004, Senegal

2004.assessment:
Civil liberties at good level. Casamance peace accord improved liberties
in south. Press, civil society, assembly rights all well-protected.
Senegal a model for democratic liberties in West Africa. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Senegal

2005.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained at good level under mid-Wade period. Free press,
civil society, religious freedom all functioning. Some concerns about
executive tendency toward personalism but civil liberties broadly protected.
FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Senegal

2006.assessment:
Civil liberties at good level in 2006. Vibrant press, civil society
active, religious freedom strong (Mouridiyya and Tijaniyya brotherhoods).
Senegal maintained Free status. Some harassment of journalists a concern
but overall liberties well-protected. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Senegal

2007.assessment:
Civil liberties at good level in 2007 (election year). Presidential
election campaign was open with diverse candidate participation and
press coverage. Wade re-elected in first round 55% but opposition competed
freely. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Senegal

2008.assessment:
Civil liberties at good but declining level in 2008. Food price protests
managed with some heavy-handedness. Wade's governance style increasingly
personalistic but civil liberties formally protected. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Senegal

2009.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate-good level in 2009. Free press operating.
Political opposition won significant local election gains. Some press
restrictions and media concentration under state influence. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Senegal

2010.assessment:
Civil liberties declined as Wade attempted constitutional manipulation.
FH downgraded Senegal from Free to Partly Free in this period. Growing
executive pressure on judiciary and press. Some political arrests and
harassment of opponents. FH CL=4 (inverted: 57.1).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Senegal

2011.assessment:
Civil liberties under stress in 2011. June 2011 protests (against Wade's
constitutional rule change) were met with teargas and some arrests. But
protests were permitted, and Constitutional Council independence maintained.
Civil liberties partially free. FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Senegal

2012.assessment:
Civil liberties improved in 2012 with Sall's election. FH restored Free
status to Senegal. Wade departed without reprisals. Free press, political
assembly, civil society all restored to full function. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Senegal

2013.assessment:
Civil liberties well-maintained under early Sall. Free press, civil
society, assembly rights all protected. Prosecution of Karim Wade raised
concerns about opposition harassment but overall liberties good. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Senegal

2014.assessment:
Civil liberties at good level. Free press, civil society, religious
freedom all functioning. Acte III decentralization expanded local rights.
Some concerns about Sall's prosecution of opposition but broad civil
liberties protected. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Senegal

2015.assessment:
Civil liberties at good level in 2015-2016. Constitutional referendum
(2016) held in free environment. Press freedom, assembly, civil society
all functioning. Senegal among Africa's best civil liberty performers.
FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Senegal

2016.assessment:
Civil liberties at good level following constitutional referendum.
Press freedom, assembly, civil society all active. Some concerns about
media concentration and influence of powerful business interests.
Overall FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Senegal

2017.assessment:
Civil liberties at good level in 2017. Legislative elections open and
competitive. Press freedom maintained though some concerns. Religious
freedom, assembly rights all functioning. FH CL=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Senegal

2018.assessment:
Civil liberties declining somewhat in 2018. Prosecution of opposition
politicians (Khalifa Sall, Karim Wade) seen as politically motivated.
Some journalists faced pressure. Assembly rights generally respected but
political opposition faced barriers. FH CL=3 but approaching downgrade.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Senegal

2019.assessment:
Civil liberties declined in 2019. FH downgraded Senegal from Free to
Partly Free, citing exclusion of opposition candidates through prosecutions.
Press freedom declining. Political assembly sometimes restricted. FH CL=4
(inverted: 57.1).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Senegal

2020.assessment:
Civil liberties at moderate level in 2020. COVID emergency restrictions
imposed but lifted relatively quickly. Opposition space narrowing. Press
under pressure. FH CL=4. Partly Free status maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Senegal

2021.assessment:
Civil liberties under strain in 2021. Sonko arrest triggered protests
with 10-14 deaths in police clashes. Internet shutdowns deployed during
unrest. Journalists arrested. Government used emergency law. FH CL=4
but declining. Partly Free status.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Senegal

2022.assessment:
Civil liberties at strained level in 2022. Continued opposition prosecutions.
Some internet restrictions during protests. Legislative elections competitive
but political space narrowing. Partly Free. FH CL=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Senegal

2023.assessment:
Civil liberties at most constrained level in 2023. Sonko convicted,
election postponed, major protests with deaths, internet shutdowns, mass
arrests. FH downgraded further. Significant restrictions on assembly,
press, and political participation. FH CL=5 (inverted: 42.9).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Senegal

2024.assessment:
Civil liberties improving in 2024 with the peaceful election and transfer
of power to Faye. Political prisoners released. Press freedom improving.
FH improved somewhat but Senegal remained Partly Free. CL=4 (inverted: 57.1).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Senegal

2025.assessment:
Civil liberties continuing to improve under new Faye government in 2025.
Faye released political detainees and signaled commitment to press freedom.
But institutional habits from Sall era take time to reverse. Partly Free
status likely maintained with improving trend. FH CL=4 estimated.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Senegal

1990.assessment:
Senegal under Diouf in 1990 had a dominant PS (Socialist Party) with
moderate internal cohesion. The PS had ruled since independence in 1960.
Some internal factionalism but the elite was unified enough to govern
effectively. Opposition existed but was not a serious threat yet.
Wade's PDS was the main opposition force.

sources.citation: Quantitative analysis of Senegalese politics; Freedom House 1991

1991.assessment:
PS elite cohesion stable in 1991. Diouf brought some opposition leaders
into government in a unity government arrangement. Wade's PDS gained
experience in government briefly. Coalition management kept elite
competition at manageable levels.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1992, Senegal

1992.assessment:
Elite competition intensifying as elections approached. Wade left the
unity government in 1992 to campaign against Diouf. Multiple opposition
parties coalescing. PS internal tensions as successor question emerged.
Competition managed within democratic framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1993, Senegal

1993.assessment:
Elite competition peaked in 1993 elections. Diouf re-elected (58%)
against multiple candidates including Wade. Results disputed by opposition
but accepted. PS elite somewhat fractious. PS/PDS competition managed
within formal institutional framework without violence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1994, Senegal

1994.assessment:
Post-election elite stability. CFA franc devaluation (January 1994) caused
economic stress but PS elite maintained cohesion around Diouf. Opposition
protests energized but within manageable bounds. No serious coup risk or
elite fragmentation.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995, Senegal

1995.assessment:
Elite dynamics stable in 1995. PS governing with PS internal tensions
about succession. Wade continued as main opposition figure. Elite
competition managed through democratic channels. No coup risk.
Marabout (Sufi brotherhood) leaders play stabilizing role as power brokers.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996, Senegal

1996.assessment:
Elite dynamics stable in 1996. Decentralization reform (ONEL, regional
devolution) managed competing elite interests by giving more actors
access to power. PS and opposition competing but within managed framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997, Senegal

1997.assessment:
Elite competition intensifying as 2000 elections approached. Wade
building broader coalition against Diouf/PS. PS beginning to show
cracks as the prospect of losing power became real. Competition managed
within institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998, Senegal

1998.assessment:
Elite competition intensifying in 1998. Legislative elections (May 1998)
competitive. PS maintained majority but opposition gaining. Diouf under
pressure. PS beginning to fragment as members calculated post-Diouf
futures. Competition managed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999, Senegal

1999.assessment:
Elite defections from PS accelerating in 1999 as Wade's coalition grew.
Moustapha Niasse (AFP) and other PS figures joined Wade coalition.
PS losing cohesion as election neared. Critical mass of elite defections
made PS defeat possible. Managed competition becoming open factional conflict.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000, Senegal

2000.assessment:
Elite cohesion transformed in 2000. Wade won presidency with broad
coalition (PDS + Niasse AFP + others). Old PS ruling elite accepted
defeat. Diouf departed peacefully. New governing coalition somewhat
heterogeneous but united around Wade. Factional competition resolved
through elections — back to manageable competition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001, Senegal

2001.assessment:
Wade governing with broad but heterogeneous coalition. March 2001
legislative elections gave Wade's Sopi coalition strong majority.
Internal coalition tensions but Wade managed them. PS in opposition
but accepting defeat gracefully. Elite cohesion functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002, Senegal

2002.assessment:
Wade governing with some internal coalition tensions. Some early allies
beginning to distance themselves. But overall governing coalition
coherent around Wade's leadership. PS regrouping in opposition.
Minor tensions but functional governance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003, Senegal

2003.assessment:
Wade governing with coalition showing signs of strain. Niasse (AFP) left
the governing coalition in 2004. Wade pursuing increasingly personalistic
governance. Some ministers marginalized. But no serious fracture yet.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004, Senegal

2004.assessment:
Coalition tensions increased in 2004. Niasse (AFP) exited governing
coalition. Macky Sall was Interior Minister and remained loyal. Idrissa
Seck (former PM) fell out with Wade. Coalition fraying but Wade maintaining
control. Competition managed but tensions growing.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Senegal

2005.assessment:
Elite tensions high in 2005-2006. Wade dismissed PM Idrissa Seck who
was then arrested on corruption charges (2005). Macky Sall appointed PM.
Multiple elite defections as Wade's inner circle narrowed. Competition
intensifying but managed within political framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Senegal

2006.assessment:
Elite competition at significant level in 2006. Multiple former allies
in opposition (Niasse, Seck). Macky Sall PM but growing tension with
Wade. Growing opposition coalition forming ahead of 2007 elections.
Competition intensifying but within democratic framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Senegal

2007.assessment:
Wade re-elected first round (55%) in 2007 despite broad opposition
coalition. Macky Sall fell out with Wade after 2007 elections (dismissed
2008). Wade narrowing coalition to family and loyalists. Elite competition
managed but becoming personalized.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Senegal

2008.assessment:
Elite fractures deepening in 2008. Macky Sall dismissed and founded
APR (Alliance for the Republic) in opposition. Karim Wade (Wade's son)
gaining power as anointed successor. Wade governing with diminishing
elite base. Elite competition managed but unstable.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Senegal

2009.assessment:
Elite competition intensifying around Wade's succession plan and Karim
Wade's rise. Opposition gaining local election victories (March 2009).
Wade's coalition narrowing as key figures defected. Multiple elite
factions forming against Wade.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Senegal

2010.assessment:
Elite competition at high level in 2010. Wade pursuing constitutional
manipulation to enable his son's succession or his own continued rule.
Multiple elite factions in opposition (Sall APR, Niasse AFP, Seck Rewmi,
PS). Civil society mobilizing. Competition becoming open factional conflict.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Senegal

2011.assessment:
Elite conflict at peak in 2011. June 2011 protests against Wade's
constitutional changes mobilized broad opposition including M23 movement
(civil society + opposition). Constitutional Council rejected Wade's
proposal. Elite cohesion around Wade at minimum — most major figures
had defected. Open factional conflict.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Senegal

2012.assessment:
Elite cohesion transformed in 2012. Macky Sall won second round of
presidential election (65%) defeating Wade with support of PS, AFP, and
civil society. Wade departed peacefully. New coalition (Benno Bokk Yaakaar)
formed around Sall. Elite competition resolved through elections, cohesion
restored. Significant competition managed within democracy.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Senegal

2013.assessment:
Sall governing with broad Benno Bokk Yaakaar coalition (BBY). Coalition
diverse but functional. PDS (Wade's party) in opposition but diminished.
Coalition tensions minor. Sall pursuing good governance agenda. Elite
cohesion functional with minor tensions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Senegal

2014.assessment:
BBY coalition governing with minor tensions. Sall accommodating diverse
coalition partners including PS and AFP. Some internal competition but
managed. Opposition (PDS, Sonko emerging) active but not threatening.
Elite cohesion functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Senegal

2015.assessment:
Elite cohesion functional under Sall's BBY coalition. Some internal
tensions as coalition partners jockeyed for position. Ousmane Sonko
emerging as opposition voice but not yet major figure. Competition
managed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Senegal

2016.assessment:
BBY coalition governing with minor tensions. Constitutional referendum
(2016) passed with broad support. Elite cohesion functional. Opposition
more vocal but manageable. Minor tensions within coalition but functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Senegal

2017.assessment:
BBY coalition won legislative elections (July 2017) with strong majority.
Elite cohesion functional under Sall. Sonko's Pastef party gaining
prominence. Competition managed within democratic framework. Minor
tensions but functional governance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Senegal

2018.assessment:
Elite cohesion somewhat strained as Sall increasingly used judiciary
against rivals (Khalifa Sall, Karim Wade). Some coalition partners
uncomfortable with approach. Sonko gaining support as anti-establishment
figure. Competition intensifying but managed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Senegal

2019.assessment:
Sall re-elected first round 58% after excluding Khalifa Sall and Karim
Wade as candidates through prosecution. Elite competition managed but
at cost of legitimacy concerns. BBY coalition intact. Sonko Pastef in
second place — emerging opposition force.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Senegal

2020.assessment:
Elite competition increasing. Sonko's Pastef party gaining popular support.
BBY coalition having internal tensions. COVID restrictions managed by Sall.
Competition manageable but Sonko's rise complicating the political landscape.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Senegal

2021.assessment:
Elite conflict escalating in 2021. Sonko arrested on rape charges (widely
seen as politically motivated), triggering major protests. Sall-Sonko
conflict became a defining political rupture. BBY coalition somewhat
divided on approach. Open factional conflict between government and
opposition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Senegal

2022.assessment:
Elite conflict continued in 2022. Legislative elections (June 2022) —
Sall's BBY lost parliamentary majority, Sonko's Yewwi coalition gained
strongly. BBY now governing without majority. Major constitutional/political
tension. Open factional conflict between ruling coalition and opposition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Senegal

2023.assessment:
Elite conflict at peak in 2023. Sall postponed election, Sonko convicted,
protests killed 15-20 people. Constitutional Council overruled Sall.
Multiple elite factions in open conflict. Active power struggles with
serious constitutional implications.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Senegal

2024.assessment:
Elite conflict resolved through elections in 2024. Faye (Sonko-backed)
won convincingly, third peaceful transfer. Sall accepted defeat and
departed. New Pastef government began governance. Elite competition
significant but managed within democratic framework once again.

sources.citation: Election observation reports, Senegal March 2024

2025.assessment:
New Faye/Pastef government governing with majority in parliament (won
November 2024 legislative elections with large majority). Sall/BBY in
opposition. Early days of new government — cohesion around Faye/Sonko.
Competition managed within democratic framework.

sources.citation: Senegal legislative elections November 2024

1990.assessment:
Senegal in 1990 was a functioning multiparty state under President Diouf
(PS party, Socialist Party). Senegal had maintained multiparty elections
since independence with the PS dominant. Institutions including judiciary,
parliament, and electoral commission functioned, though not independently
— the PS controlled all levers. Partially functional democratic state.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1991, Senegal

1991.assessment:
Institutions functioning at same level as 1990. Senegal maintained its
tradition of formal multiparty democracy with parliamentary elections
though PS dominance continued. Courts and parliament functional but
not fully independent.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1992, Senegal

1992.assessment:
Institutional functioning similar to prior years under Diouf. New
electoral code being developed. Multiparty system more competitive
as opposition parties grew. Courts and electoral commission improving
though still not fully independent.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1993, Senegal

1993.assessment:
Presidential election in February 1993 (Diouf re-elected, officially
58%) was disputed by opposition but deemed legitimate. Electoral commission
contested but functioning. Courts active. Parliament competitive but
PS-dominated. Partially functional institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1994, Senegal

1994.assessment:
Institutional functioning continued at moderate level. CFA franc devaluation
(January 1994) caused protests. Government managing through existing
institutions. Electoral commission reform underway. Partially functional
state with dominant party characteristics.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995, Senegal

1995.assessment:
Institutions improving slightly. PS and opposition parties reached
agreement on electoral reform. Independent National Electoral Commission
(CENI) being discussed. Parliament competitive. Courts not fully independent
but functioning. Senegal's institutions above African average.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996, Senegal

1996.assessment:
Significant institutional reform in 1996. New Independent Electoral
Commission (ONEL) established. Regional elections decentralized power.
Institutional improvement toward a more genuinely multiparty state.
Courts gaining more independence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997, Senegal

1997.assessment:
Institutions functioning better following 1996 electoral commission reform.
Legislative elections (1998) being prepared with improved institutional
framework. Judiciary gaining confidence. Senegal's democratic institutions
improving but still with flaws.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998, Senegal

1998.assessment:
Legislative elections (May 1998) were conducted under improved ONEL
oversight. PS maintained majority but opposition competitive. Courts
and parliament functional with some independence. Improving institutional
trajectory toward the 2000 transition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999, Senegal

1999.assessment:
Institutions functioning well in the pre-election year. ONEL and electoral
framework credible enough for all parties to participate. Courts independent
enough to adjudicate electoral disputes. Parliament active. Foundation
for peaceful transfer laid.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000, Senegal

2000.assessment:
In 2000, Senegalese institutions passed the democratic test: the first
transfer of power since independence was achieved peacefully. ONEL managed
credible elections. Courts upheld results. Wade government inaugurated
without incident. Demonstrates functional_but_flawed institutions working
at critical moment.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001, Senegal

2001.assessment:
Institutions consolidating under Wade. New constitution (January 2001)
strengthened presidential powers but maintained democratic framework.
CENA (Autonomous Electoral Commission) established. Parliament with
effective opposition following March 2001 legislative elections.
Functional democratic institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002, Senegal

2002.assessment:
Institutions functioning under Wade government. Some concerns about
creeping executive dominance. Courts and parliament active. CENA managing
elections credibly. Institutional functioning at functional_but_flawed level.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003, Senegal

2003.assessment:
Institutions functioning under Wade with some concerns about presidential
dominance and attempts to centralize power. Parliament and courts active.
Democratic institutions functional with growing concerns about Wade's
governance style.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004, Senegal

2004.assessment:
Institutions continuing under Wade. Courts and parliament functioning.
Wade's tendency to govern by personal preference rather than institutional
norms a concern, but formal institutions intact. Functional_but_flawed
adequately describes this period.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Senegal

2005.assessment:
Institutional functioning under Wade showing some signs of erosion.
Growing executive dominance, some judicial appointments politically
motivated. Parliament maintaining some independence. Overall still
functional multiparty democratic institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Senegal

2006.assessment:
Institutions under Wade with continued concerns about executive dominance.
Parliament active; courts functioning. Some problematic use of state
resources for Wade's political campaigns. Preparing for 2007 elections.
Functional_but_flawed institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Senegal

2007.assessment:
Presidential election (February 2007, Wade re-elected first round 55%)
seen as largely credible though opposition contested. Institutions
functional. Courts and parliament operating. CENA credible. Some
concerns about executive dominance intensifying.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Senegal

2008.assessment:
Institutional quality declining somewhat under late-Wade period. Wade
increasingly governing through decrees and bypassing parliament. Courts
under executive pressure. Parliament less effective as opposition boycotted
some sessions. Functional_but_flawed with deteriorating trend.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Senegal

2009.assessment:
Continued institutional concerns under Wade. Local elections (March 2009)
delivered significant opposition gains — CENA performed credibly. Courts
and parliament still functional. Executive dominance remains a concern
but institutions survive.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Senegal

2010.assessment:
Institutional quality challenged in 2010-2011 as Wade pursued constitutional
changes to install his son Karim as successor. Parliament and courts under
increasing pressure. But institutions still functioning — parliament rejected
some Wade initiatives, courts issued some independent rulings.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Senegal

2011.assessment:
Constitutional crisis in June 2011 when Wade attempted to change electoral
rules. Constitutional Council rejected the proposal — demonstrating some
institutional independence. Major test of institutions; they held, narrowly.
Protests in Dakar demonstrated civil society resilience.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Senegal

2012.assessment:
Institutions restored credibility in 2012 when Macky Sall defeated Wade
in a peaceful election. Constitutional Court, CENA, and broader institutions
demonstrated resilience. Second peaceful transfer since 2000. Institutions
functional with commitment to norms.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Senegal

2013.assessment:
Institutional quality improving under early Sall. He established CREIL
(asset recovery) and made governance reform pledges. Parliament active,
courts more independent. Sall initially improved democratic governance
norms.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Senegal

2014.assessment:
Institutional quality solid in 2014 under Sall. Electoral commission
credible. Courts active. Parliament competitive. Decentralization reform
(Acte III) further devolved power. Senegal's institutions among the better
functioning in West Africa.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Senegal

2015.assessment:
Institutions functioning well in 2015. Courts independent. Parliament
competitive. Some concerns about Sall building his own political dominance
but institutions maintain integrity. Functional with some flaws.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Senegal

2016.assessment:
Constitutional referendum in February 2016 passed (62%), reducing
presidential term to 5 years and introducing other reforms. Demonstrated
institutional health. Parliament, courts, and electoral commission all
functioning credibly.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Senegal

2017.assessment:
Legislative elections (July 2017) gave Sall's BBY coalition strong
majority. CENA managed elections credibly. Parliament and courts functional.
Some concerns about opposition space but institutions overall functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Senegal

2018.assessment:
Institutions functioning with growing concerns about Sall's use of the
judiciary against opponents. Prosecution of Khalifa Sall (Dakar mayor)
and Karim Wade seen as politically motivated. Courts beginning to be used
instrumentally. Functional_but_flawed with declining trajectory.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Senegal

2019.assessment:
Institutions functional but concerns growing. Presidential election
(February 2019) Sall re-elected 58% first round but two potential
opposition candidates had been excluded through prosecution. CENA
managed elections. Functional_but_flawed with more serious flaws emerging.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Senegal

2020.assessment:
Institutions functioning under Sall's second term. COVID-19 emergency
managed through executive action. Parliament and courts continued to
operate. Some institutional concerns about executive dominance and
limits on opposition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Senegal

2021.assessment:
Institutional stress in 2021. Sonko arrested in March on politically
contested charges; protests with deaths showed state's heavy-handed
response. Parliament still functioning. Courts increasingly seen as
executive tools. Functional_but_flawed with increasing concern.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Senegal

2022.assessment:
Institutional functioning under strain. Legislative elections (June 2022)
saw Sall lose parliamentary majority — demonstrating some institutional
integrity in electoral process. But judiciary increasingly used against
opposition. Parliament more competitive but government-opposition conflict high.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Senegal

2023.assessment:
Major institutional crisis in 2023. Sall postponed the presidential
election triggering protests and deaths. Constitutional Council overruled
Sall and ordered election to proceed. This was a critical test of institutions
— they ultimately held but the crisis revealed institutional vulnerability.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Senegal

2024.assessment:
Institutions restored credibility in 2024. Presidential election held
in March after Constitutional Council overruled postponement attempt.
Faye/Sonko opposition won. Third peaceful transfer of power. Institutions
proven resilient once more. Return to functional_but_flawed baseline.

sources.citation: Election observation reports, Senegal March 2024

2025.assessment:
New Faye government consolidating under Sonko as Prime Minister.
Institutional functioning being reset after 2023 crisis. Parliament
competitive. Courts expected to regain independence. Early period of
new government — functional with uncertainties about new governance style.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Senegal

1990.assessment:
Casamance conflict (MFDC insurgency) caused moderate violence in 1990.
Military clashes between Senegalese army and MFDC rebels in southern
Casamance. Estimated 100-300 deaths from conflict-related violence. No
violence outside Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

1991.assessment:
Casamance conflict at moderate level in 1991. Ceasefire signed but quickly
broke down. Military clashes continued. Estimated 100-200 deaths. Political
violence in Dakar minimal — Senegal was a functioning multiparty state
under Diouf.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

1992.assessment:
Major military offensives in 1992 against MFDC positions in Casamance.
Estimated 200-400 deaths from military operations and clashes. Peak
period of armed conflict in Casamance. Political violence elsewhere in
Senegal minimal.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

1993.assessment:
July 1993 Ziguinchor accord reduced violence significantly. Deaths declined
from 1992 peak. Some residual violence as ceasefire imperfectly implemented.
Estimated around 100 deaths. Political violence in presidential elections
(Diouf re-elected) minimal.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Report: Senegal's Casamance Conflict, 2003

1994.assessment:
Casamance conflict re-escalated in 1994 after peace talks broke down.
MFDC attacks on civilians and military. CFA franc devaluation (January
1994) caused urban protests in Dakar and other cities — some violence
though not major. Combined conflict-related deaths estimated 150-250.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

1995.assessment:
Casamance conflict at lower intensity in 1995. December 1995 ceasefire.
Estimated 50-150 deaths from conflict. Political violence in Senegal
outside Casamance minimal. Diouf government stable.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

1996.assessment:
Casamance conflict at low level in 1996 following 1995 ceasefire. Sporadic
attacks but no major military operations. Estimated under 100 deaths.
Political violence in Senegal generally absent outside Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

1997.assessment:
Casamance conflict re-escalated significantly in 1997-1998 with MFDC
attacks on villages and military. Major military operations by Senegalese
forces. Estimated 150-250 deaths. Violence concentrated in Casamance
forest zone.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

1998.assessment:
Casamance conflict at elevated level in 1998. Military operations
continued. Landmine incidents added to casualties. Estimated 150-250
deaths. MFDC increasingly divided into factions.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

1999.assessment:
Casamance conflict declining in 1999 as peace talks resumed. Estimated
50-150 deaths. Presidential election campaign (Diouf vs. Wade) generated
political tensions in Dakar and other cities but minimal violence.
Relatively peaceful transition period.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2000.assessment:
Political violence very low in the 2000 regime change year. The election
that brought Wade to power was largely peaceful despite being the first
transfer of power since independence. Casamance at low intensity. Estimated
under 100 deaths total. A model democratic transition.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal; election observation reports 2000

2001.assessment:
Political violence minimal in 2001. New constitutional referendum passed
peacefully. Casamance at low intensity with Wade peace initiative. Joola
ferry disaster in September 2002 (1,800 deaths) was a maritime accident,
not political violence. Overall conflict deaths estimated under 100.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2002.assessment:
Casamance conflict at low level in 2002. MFDC increasingly fragmented.
Wade government pursuing peace process. Political violence in broader
Senegal minimal. Overall conflict deaths estimated under 100.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2003.assessment:
Casamance at low violence level. MFDC faction splits reduced military
capability. Government making progress in peace process. Estimated under
100 conflict deaths. Political system functioning peacefully.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2004.assessment:
December 2004 Ziguinchor peace accord dramatically reduced Casamance
violence. Very few conflict-related deaths in 2004. Senegal at peace
for the first time in over 20 years. Political system functioning
without violence.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Briefing: Senegal's Casamance, December 2004

2005.assessment:
Post-peace accord: very few conflict deaths in 2005. Rogue MFDC factions
responsible for sporadic incidents but not organized violence. Senegal
at near-peace. Political system functioning without violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2006.assessment:
Very low conflict violence in 2006. Post-accord Casamance stability.
Rogue faction occasional attacks. Political violence in Senegal absent
outside Casamance. Estimated under 50 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2007.assessment:
Casamance conflict flared slightly in 2007 with some MFDC attacks.
Overall low violence. Presidential election in February 2007 (Wade re-elected)
was largely peaceful with some post-election protests. Overall deaths
under 100.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2008.assessment:
Low conflict violence in 2008. Casamance MFDC attacks sporadic. Political
violence in broader Senegal absent. Food price crisis (2008 global) caused
protests but minimal fatalities. Overall conflict deaths estimated under 50.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2009.assessment:
Very low violence in 2009. Casamance at minimal intensity. Political
system functioning without violence. Estimated under 50 conflict deaths.
Senegal remained stable under Wade government.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2010.assessment:
Low conflict violence in 2010. Casamance at minimal level. MFDC attacks
sporadic but not organized. Wade government preparing for 2012 elections.
Political tensions around constitutional term limits emerging but no violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2011.assessment:
Casamance conflict flared in 2011. More MFDC attacks. Major protests in
Dakar (June 2011) against Wade's constitutional manipulation — some violence
from clashes between protesters and police, several deaths. Combined
political and conflict violence estimated under 100.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC; HRW Senegal 2012

2012.assessment:
Pre-election violence in early 2012 as protesters objected to Wade's
candidacy for a third term. Several deaths in clashes between police
and protesters in January-February. Election itself (Macky Sall won)
was peaceful. Casamance at low level. Total deaths estimated under 100.

sources.citation: HRW World Report 2013, Senegal; UCDP 2012

2013.assessment:
Political violence very low in 2013 under new Sall government. Casamance
at minimal level with new peace initiative. No major political violence.
Estimated under 50 deaths from all conflict. Senegal stabilizing well.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/MFDC

2014.assessment:
Political violence at minimum in 2014. Casamance at historic low violence.
MFDC essentially inactive militarily. No significant political violence.
Senegal at peace throughout its territory.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal

2015.assessment:
Near-zero conflict violence in 2015. Senegal at peace. Casamance dormant.
No political violence of significance. Senegal's conflict era essentially
over.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal

2016.assessment:
Minimal political violence in 2016. Constitutional referendum (February
2016) passed peacefully reducing presidential term to 5 years. Casamance
dormant. Senegal a model of peaceful democratic governance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal

2017.assessment:
Minimal conflict violence in 2017. Legislative elections (July 2017)
gave Sall's coalition a strong majority — peaceful. Casamance dormant.
Senegal at peace.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal

2018.assessment:
Minimal political violence in 2018. Senegal peaceful and stable. Casamance
at peace. Gas field development (GTA) announced. No significant conflict.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal

2019.assessment:
Presidential election (February 2019, Sall re-elected first round 58%)
was peaceful. Casamance at peace. No significant political violence.
Senegal remained a stable democracy.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal

2020.assessment:
Minimal violence in 2020. COVID-19 pandemic managed with some restrictions.
Some protests against COVID measures but minimal fatalities. Casamance
at peace. Senegal stable.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal

2021.assessment:
Political violence elevated in March 2021 when opposition leader Ousmane
Sonko was arrested on rape charges seen as politically motivated. Protests
erupted across Senegal, with 10-14 deaths in clashes between police and
protesters. Significant increase from previous years but still low in
absolute terms.

sources.citation: HRW World Report 2022, Senegal; UCDP 2021

2022.assessment:
Continued political tensions in 2022 around Sonko prosecution and Sall's
potential third-term bid. Some protests and clashes. Legislative elections
(June 2022) saw Sall's coalition lose majority — peaceful but contentious.
Overall violence remained low.

sources.citation: HRW World Report 2023, Senegal; UCDP 2022

2023.assessment:
Political violence escalated in May-June 2023 when Sonko convicted of
corrupting youth and Sall postponed the presidential election (February
2024). Protests caused deaths — estimated 15-20 killed. Sall reversed
the election postponement. Serious political crisis but not armed
insurgency; fatalities below 100.

sources.citation: HRW World Report 2024, Senegal; Amnesty International 2023

2024.assessment:
Violence returned to minimal levels in 2024 after the election crisis
was resolved. Presidential election held in March 2024 was peaceful.
Bassirou Diomaye Faye (Sonko-aligned opposition) won 54% in first round.
Third peaceful transfer of power. Casamance at peace.

sources.citation: Election observation reports, Senegal March 2024

2025.assessment:
Political violence at minimal level in 2025 under new Faye government.
No armed conflict. Casamance at peace. Senegal stable under new
administration, which has asserted some anti-corruption and sovereignty
positions.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal 2025

1990.assessment:
Senegal in 1990 maintained control of most territory but the Casamance
region in the south was increasingly contested by the MFDC (Movement of
Democratic Forces of Casamance) which launched an armed insurgency in 1982.
Northern, central, and eastern Senegal were fully under government control.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Report: Senegal's Casamance Conflict, 2003

1991.assessment:
The Casamance conflict continued with occasional military clashes between
the Senegalese army and MFDC rebels. A ceasefire was signed in 1991 but
quickly broke down. The rest of Senegal remained fully under government
control.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

1992.assessment:
Casamance conflict intensified in 1992 with major military offensives by
the Senegalese army against MFDC positions. Sporadic fighting in the
Casamance forest zone. Central government maintained full control outside
Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

1993.assessment:
Peace agreement signed in July 1993 (the Ziguinchor accord) between
government and MFDC. Relative calm in Casamance though full MFDC
disarmament did not occur. Government maintained control of main towns
and infrastructure.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Report: Senegal's Casamance Conflict, 2003

1994.assessment:
Casamance conflict resumed in 1994 after peace talks broke down. MFDC
split into factions. Government forces maintained control of Ziguinchor
city and main roads but rural forest areas of Casamance remained contested.
No threat to territorial control outside of Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

1995.assessment:
Casamance conflict at relatively low intensity in 1995. A new ceasefire
in December 1995. MFDC factionalization reduced its military capacity.
Government controlled all urban centers and major infrastructure. Rural
Casamance forest areas intermittently contested.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Report: Senegal's Casamance Conflict, 2003

1996.assessment:
Casamance conflict at low intensity in 1996 following December 1995
ceasefire. Some sporadic attacks but overall reduced violence. Government
presence in Casamance strengthened somewhat. Outside Casamance full
government control maintained.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

1997.assessment:
Casamance conflict escalated again in 1997-1998, with the MFDC carrying
out attacks and mine-laying. The forest regions of Casamance (Ziguinchor,
Kolda departments) remained insecure. Government controlled towns and roads
but forest areas less secure.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

1998.assessment:
Casamance conflict continued at elevated intensity. Landmine presence
significant in rural Casamance. Government launched military operations.
MFDC factions increasingly fractured. Northern Senegal and Dakar completely
peaceful and under full government control.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Report: Senegal's Casamance Conflict, 2003

1999.assessment:
Casamance conflict at lower intensity in 1999, approaching the regime
change year. President Diouf engaged in peace negotiations. Some MFDC
factions more open to peace. Government controlled all urban areas and
main infrastructure while rural Casamance forests remained contested.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2000.assessment:
In 2000, the regime change year, Wade came to power and launched new peace
initiatives for Casamance. Overall conflict intensity low. The Casamance
insurgency remained active but at low level. Government had full control
of all areas outside the Casamance forest zone.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Report: Senegal's Casamance Conflict, 2003

2001.assessment:
Wade pursued peace in Casamance with renewed energy. A ceasefire was
negotiated. Conflict at low intensity. Government controlled all urban
centers and main infrastructure. Casamance forest areas partially contested.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2002.assessment:
Peace process in Casamance continued under Wade. Casamance conflict
significantly reduced. A major peace agreement was signed in December
2004 (Ziguinchor agreement) was being prepared. MFDC increasingly
fragmented, reducing coherent military activity.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Report: Senegal's Casamance Conflict, 2003

2003.assessment:
Casamance conflict at low intensity in 2003. MFDC fragmented into multiple
factions with different levels of commitment to armed struggle. Government
invested in Casamance infrastructure and development. Rural Casamance
forest zones intermittently insecure.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2004.assessment:
A comprehensive peace agreement was signed in Ziguinchor in December 2004
between the government and key MFDC factions. The most significant peace
accord of the Casamance conflict. Violence dropped sharply. Government
control of Casamance significantly expanded.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Briefing: Senegal's Casamance, December 2004

2005.assessment:
Following the 2004 peace accord, Casamance conflict at very low intensity
in 2005. Some rogue MFDC factions continued sporadic attacks but main
military faction inactive. Government rebuilding presence in formerly
contested areas. Full control throughout most of Senegal.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2006.assessment:
Casamance conflict at very low intensity. The 2004 peace accord largely
holding. Occasional attacks by rogue MFDC factions but not coordinated
insurgency. Government had near-full control throughout Senegal including
most of Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2007.assessment:
Casamance violence flared slightly in 2007 as MFDC factions rearmed.
But overall conflict level remained low. Government controlled all urban
areas and main roads. Forest border areas of Casamance remained somewhat
contested.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2008.assessment:
Casamance conflict continued at low level in 2008. Attacks on military
positions and civilians in the forest zone. MFDC factions increasingly
criminalized (timber smuggling, cannabis cultivation). Government maintained
full control outside Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2009.assessment:
Casamance conflict at low intensity in 2009. MFDC fragmented further.
Government maintained control of urban areas and main roads. Rural forest
areas along Guinea-Bissau border remained insecure. No challenge to
territorial control outside of southern Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2010.assessment:
Casamance conflict at low but persistent intensity in 2010. Attacks on
civilians and military. MFDC deeply fragmented. Government control of
Casamance urban centers solid. Rural forest areas along Guinea-Bissau and
Guinea borders intermittently contested.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2011.assessment:
Casamance conflict erupted more significantly in 2011-2012, with MFDC
attacks on military convoys and villages. The Forest of Carabane and
Sindian areas saw increased activity. Government maintained full control
outside Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2012.assessment:
In 2012, the democratic transition from Wade to Macky Sall brought new
hope for Casamance peace. Violence in Casamance was at low-moderate level
in early 2012 before declining after Sall's election. Sall immediately
prioritized Casamance peace negotiations.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2013.assessment:
Casamance conflict declining under Sall. New ceasefire negotiations
underway. Government strengthened military operations against rogue factions.
MFDC's Salif Sadio faction retreated toward Guinea-Bissau. Overall
security in Casamance improving.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2014.assessment:
Casamance conflict reached its lowest point in decades in 2014. UCDP
records minimal organized violence. MFDC factions increasingly inactive
or engaged in criminal activities rather than insurgency. Government
control of Casamance significantly expanded.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2015.assessment:
Casamance at very low conflict intensity. Government pursuing development
investment in Casamance. MFDC inactive as a military force. Senegal
had effective control of the entire national territory.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2016.assessment:
Casamance conflict essentially dormant. Very few MFDC-attributed incidents.
Landmines remain a legacy issue but not active conflict. Senegal had
full effective territorial control. New gas discoveries offshore announced.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2017.assessment:
Casamance conflict at minimal level. MFDC essentially militarily inactive.
Government full control of territory. Demining operations ongoing in
formerly affected areas. Senegal stable and fully controlled.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2018.assessment:
Full territorial control throughout Senegal. Casamance conflict essentially
over as an organized armed insurgency, though legacy issues (landmines,
displaced persons) remain. Government pursuing development programs
in Casamance.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2019.assessment:
Full territorial control throughout Senegal. Casamance at peace. Oil and
gas development in offshore areas proceeding. Political tensions around
constitutional term limits but no territorial challenges to state authority.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2020.assessment:
Full territorial control throughout Senegal. Casamance conflict dormant.
COVID-19 pandemic managed by central government with effective state
presence throughout the country. No territorial challenges.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2021.assessment:
Full territorial control throughout Senegal. Political protests (Sonko
affair) led to civil unrest in Dakar and other cities but did not
challenge government territorial authority. Casamance remained at peace.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2022.assessment:
Full territorial control throughout Senegal. Political tensions around
Sall's potential third term and opposition prosecutions continued, with
protests in urban areas. These were political disputes, not territorial
challenges. Casamance at peace.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2023.assessment:
Full territorial control throughout Senegal. Political crisis intensified
as Sall arrested Sonko and announced election postponement, triggering
major protests with deaths. Central government authority maintained.
No armed insurgency or territorial challenge.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal/Casamance

2024.assessment:
Full territorial control throughout Senegal. Election held in March 2024
after controversy; Bassirou Diomaye Faye (Sonko-aligned) won in first
round with 54%. Third peaceful transfer of power. Casamance at peace.
Full government authority maintained throughout transition.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal; election results 2024

2025.assessment:
Full territorial control throughout Senegal under new Faye government.
Casamance at peace. Senegal stable with new government pursuing its
program. No armed challenges to territorial authority.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Senegal

1990.assessment:
Budget transparency in Senegal in 1990 was minimal by modern standards. The Open
Budget Survey did not begin until 2006. During the Diouf era, budget documents were
produced but public access was limited. Parliamentary scrutiny existed but the PS
party's dominance of the legislature reduced effective oversight. Citizens had little
access to fiscal information. Based on regional comparisons and institutional context,
OBI-equivalent estimated at approximately 15-20 (scant).

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership pre-OBS assessments; World Bank PEFA framework

1991.assessment:
Budget transparency remained minimal under the Diouf government. Estimated OBI-equivalent
around 15-20 (scant). Limited public budget documents.

sources.citation: Retrospective assessment based on IBP pre-OBS documentation

1992.assessment: Budget transparency remained minimal. Estimated OBI-equivalent around 15-20 (scant).

sources.citation: Retrospective assessment based on IBP pre-OBS documentation

1993.assessment:
Budget transparency remained minimal. IMF structural adjustment engagement began
requiring more fiscal reporting, slightly improving transparency of public accounts.
Estimated OBI-equivalent 18-22 (scant to minimal).

sources.citation: IMF Senegal program documentation 1993; IBP retrospective

1994.assessment:
IMF/World Bank structural adjustment conditionality following CFA franc devaluation
began requiring more transparent fiscal reporting. Estimated OBI-equivalent 20-25

sources.citation: IMF Senegal post-devaluation program documentation 1994

Gradual improvement in fiscal transparency under IMF structural adjustment programs.

Gradual improvement under continued IMF engagement. The introduction of UEMOA

OBS 2006 (first survey) scored Senegal at 44 out of 100 — limited budget transparency.

consistently published. Wade government provided some budget transparency but
procurement and off-budget spending remained opaque.

Estimated OBI approximately 44 (limited), consistent with 2006. OBS not conducted

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, OBS 2006/2008 interpolation

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, OBS 2008/2010 interpolation

OBS 2010 scored Senegal at 47 — limited budget transparency. Modest improvement

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2010

OBS 2012 scored Senegal at 48 — limited budget transparency. Sall's election

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2012

Estimated OBI approximately 50 (limited), improving under Sall government's reform

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, OBS 2012/2015 interpolation

Estimated OBI approximately 52 (limited). Sall government's Plan Sénégal Emergent

OBS 2015 scored Senegal at 54 — limited budget transparency. Meaningful improvement

2017.assessment:
OBS 2017 scored Senegal at 53 — limited budget transparency. Marginal dip from

OBS 2019 scored Senegal at 56 — limited budget transparency, approaching the

2020.assessment:
Estimated OBI approximately 56 (limited). COVID-19 emergency spending introduced

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, OBS 2019/2021 interpolation

OBS 2021 scored Senegal at 51 — limited budget transparency. Drop from 2019
