sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2021

2022.assessment:
Estimated OBI approximately 51 (limited), consistent with 2021. Concerns about

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, OBS 2021/2023 interpolation

2023.assessment:
OBS 2023 scored Senegal at 49 — limited budget transparency. The election crisis
and political tensions of 2023 may have affected governance quality. More significantly,
the Faye government's 2024 audit would later reveal that Sall had hidden approximately

figures were materially misleading to the public.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2023

Faye government disclosed the hidden Sall-era debt publicly in early 2024, an act of

opacity in the prior period. The new government committed to improved budget transparency

transition period and ongoing audit revelations.

Estimated OBI approximately 50 (limited). Faye government's transparency commitments

1990.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index not available before 2011. Senegal's legal system in 1990
was based on French civil law tradition with a formal legislative and judicial
structure, though dominated by the ruling PS party. Laws were published in the
official gazette (Journal Officiel). The formal legal architecture existed but
access to legal information was limited; judicial independence was constrained.
Estimated WJP Open Government component approximately 40-48 (partial transparency).

1990.assessment:
RSF Press Freedom Index did not begin until 2002. Senegal's press environment in
1990 under Diouf was mixed: state broadcaster RTS dominated the airwaves while a
vibrant but constrained press existed. Major newspapers like Le Soleil (state-owned)
and Le Quotidien operated; independent radio gradually emerging. Freedom House
rated Senegal's press as Partly Free in this period. Estimated RSF-equivalent
score in the 40-50 range (problematic), reflecting single-party dominance of
state media alongside some independent press.

1996.assessment:
Senegal's press sector becoming more vibrant. Growing private media landscape.
Estimated RSF-equivalent 48-55 (problematic).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press 1996
Freedom House Freedom of the Press 1997

1998.assessment:
Continued growth of private media. Press environment gradually improving.
Estimated RSF-equivalent 50-57 (problematic to satisfactory).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press 1998

1999.assessment:
Press freedom improving ahead of the 2000 election. Independent media critical of
Diouf government permitted. Estimated RSF-equivalent 52-58 (problematic to satisfactory).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press 1999

2000.assessment:
Democratic transition year. Press environment significantly improved under Wade who
promised press freedom. Private media flourished. Independent press openly covered
the election campaign. Estimated RSF-equivalent 55-62 (satisfactory).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press 2000; CPJ Africa records 2000

2001.assessment:
Press freedom improved under Wade. Independent media vibrant.
Estimated RSF-equivalent 58-65 (satisfactory).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press 2001

2002.assessment:
First RSF index year. Senegal scored relatively well (RSF ~37, inverted 63 = satisfactory).
Under Wade, Senegal enjoyed vibrant press freedom with many independent newspapers,
radio stations (including RFM, Walf FM, Sud FM), and online media. Some defamation
laws remained on the books but were seldom used aggressively.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2002

2003.assessment:
Press freedom remained good. RSF inverted approximately 62 (satisfactory).
Independent media active and critical of government where appropriate.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2003

2004.assessment:
Good press freedom environment. RSF inverted approximately 61 (satisfactory).
Senegal ranked among West Africa's freest press environments.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2004

2005.assessment:
Good press freedom. RSF inverted approximately 60 (satisfactory). Some concerns
about Wade government using defamation laws against critical journalists beginning
to emerge.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2005

2006.assessment:
Press freedom slightly declining as Wade government increasingly used legal tools
against critical media. RSF inverted approximately 58 (satisfactory/problematic border).
Journalists reporting on corruption faced increasing legal harassment.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2006

2007.assessment:
Press freedom modestly declining. RSF inverted approximately 57 (problematic).
Journalists occasionally arrested and charged with defamation.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2007

2008.assessment:
Press freedom declining further under Wade's increasing authoritarianism.
RSF inverted approximately 55 (problematic). Defamation cases against journalists
rising; some broadcast licenses threatened.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2008

2009.assessment:
Press freedom continued declining. RSF inverted approximately 53 (problematic).
Journalists facing more frequent legal harassment from the Wade government.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2009

2010.assessment:
Press freedom under pressure. RSF inverted approximately 52 (problematic).
Media landscape remained vibrant with many outlets but legal threats against
critical journalists continued.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2010

2011.assessment:
Press freedom at its lowest under Wade. RSF inverted approximately 51 (problematic).
M23 protests and political unrest saw some intimidation of journalists covering
demonstrations. Wade government targeted opposition media.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2011; CPJ Africa reports 2011

2012.assessment:
Sharp improvement under Sall government. RSF inverted approximately 60 (satisfactory).
Sall government released journalists jailed by Wade, dropped defamation cases,
and publicly committed to press freedom as a democratic value. Senegal moved
significantly up RSF rankings.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2012

2013.assessment:
Good press freedom maintained. RSF inverted approximately 61 (satisfactory).
Private media vibrant with multiple daily newspapers and dozens of radio/TV stations.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2013

2014.assessment: Good press freedom maintained. RSF inverted approximately 61 (satisfactory).

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2014

2015.assessment:
Good press freedom maintained. RSF inverted approximately 60 (satisfactory).
Senegal remained a relative regional leader in press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2015

2016.assessment:
Good press freedom. RSF inverted approximately 59 (satisfactory). Some concerns
about Sall government's growing comfort using legal tools against critics, but
overall environment remained relatively open.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2016

2017.assessment:
Press freedom slightly declining. RSF inverted approximately 58 (satisfactory/problematic).
Defamation laws began being applied more frequently; some journalists targeted.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2017

2018.assessment:
Press freedom declining. RSF inverted approximately 57 (problematic). Opposition
media and journalists critical of Sall faced increasing pressure. Cybercrime law
used against online journalists.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2018

2019.assessment:
Press freedom under increasing pressure. RSF inverted approximately 56 (problematic).
Sall's second term saw more aggressive targeting of opposition media. Cybercrime
law created chilling effect on online journalists.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2019

2020.assessment:
Press freedom declining. RSF inverted approximately 55 (problematic). COVID-19
period saw some restrictions on media coverage. Sall government increasingly
using legal tools against journalists critical of its handling of Sonko case
and opposition activities.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2020

2021.assessment:
Press freedom continued declining. RSF inverted approximately 54 (problematic).
Journalists covering the Sonko protests (March 2021) faced arrests and harassment.
Social media and internet occasionally throttled during protests. International
press freedom organizations raised concerns.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2021; CPJ Africa reports 2021

2022.assessment:
Press freedom under significant pressure. RSF inverted approximately 52 (problematic).
Journalists covering opposition protests faced harassment. Multiple journalists
detained or charged. RSF ranked Senegal in 73rd position in 2022.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2022

2023.assessment:
Press freedom severely deteriorated in 2023. RSF inverted approximately 46 (problematic
approaching difficult). Journalists covering the election postponement crisis faced
arrests; internet shutdowns during protests directly targeted media access.
Journalists and bloggers were arrested for covering protests. RSF ranked Senegal
101st globally in 2023, a major drop.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2023; RSF Senegal country page 2023

2024.assessment:
Press freedom improved significantly under Faye government. RSF inverted approximately
55 (problematic, improving toward satisfactory). Faye/Sonko had promised and began
delivering improved press freedom. Some journalists released; Cybercrime law review
announced. Media reported more freely on the new government. RSF improved Senegal's
ranking.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders, Press Freedom Index 2024; RSF Senegal country page 2024

2025.assessment:
Press freedom expected to continue gradual improvement under Faye government.
Estimated RSF inverted approximately 57 (problematic to satisfactory transition).
Media sector recovering from 2023 nadir.

sources.citation: RSF 2025 estimate based on prior trend and Faye government policy commitments

1990.assessment:
World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (SCI) not available before 2004. Senegal's
statistical agency ANSD (Agence Nationale de la Statistique et de la Démographie) and
its predecessor DPS existed but capacity was limited by resources. Based on regional
context and subsequent trajectory, statistical capacity estimated at approximately
50-60 (moderate) in 1990. Population census conducted in 1988 with data available.
National accounts published with some delays.

sources.citation: World Bank retrospective assessment; ANSD institutional history

1991.assessment:
Statistical capacity moderate. Estimated SCI approximately 50-60. National accounts
and demographic data produced with some gaps.

sources.citation: World Bank retrospective assessment

Statistical capacity moderate. IMF structural adjustment engagement required
more systematic macroeconomic data. Estimated SCI approximately 52-62.

Statistical capacity moderate. CFA franc devaluation required enhanced data
collection for monetary and trade statistics. Estimated SCI approximately 52-62.

2020.assessment:
SCI score of 81 — excellent statistical capacity maintained. COVID-19 period
challenged data collection somewhat but Senegal maintained strong statistical output.
Hidden fiscal data (later revealed 2024) not yet apparent in statistical performance.

2021.assessment: SCI score of 81 — excellent statistical capacity.

2022.assessment:
SCI score of 81 — excellent statistical capacity maintained. Despite political
unrest, statistical agency operated normally.

2023.assessment:
SCI score of 80 — excellent to good border. Statistical capacity maintained.
Retrospectively, the hidden debt problem revealed by Faye government suggests
that official fiscal statistics published 2020-2023 understated actual debt,
but the methodological capacity of the statistical agency itself was not impaired.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Senegal 2023

2024.assessment:
SCI estimated at approximately 80. The Faye government's audit and debt revelation
paradoxically improved transparency by disclosing previously hidden data, while
the new government committed to international data standards. However, the restructuring
of fiscal accounts may temporarily affect statistical series continuity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator estimate 2024

2025.assessment:
SCI estimated at approximately 80. Statistical capacity expected to remain high
under new government's transparency commitments.
